content.full_text:
State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
Ted and Barbara 
Date :April 8, 1983 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Jani s McLean 
Subject: Persons acting as treasurers without actually being 
treasurers 
ATTORNEY CASES 
A. 
Goal: 
To find cases where nonlawyers did work, under an 
attorney's name, and were held liable for their 
involvement. 
B. 
Result: 
This wasn't a helpful area because the cases deal 
only with the attorney's liability for breach of 
duty, or for violating the rules against aiding and 
abetting nonlawyers in the practice of law. 
C. 
Materials Checked: 
1. 
Business & Professions Code on attorneys 
2. 
west's Cal. Digest (Attorney/Client) 
3. 
Cal. Jur. (Attorney/Client) 


ACCOUNTANT CASES 
A. 
Goal: 
To try to find cases where a filer's name is on an 
IRS filing, etc., but the actual preparer (the 
accountant) was held liable. 
B. 
Result: 
No luck. 
Accountants have a duty of care they must 
comply with and this is the basis for their 
liability. 
We do not have any duty in our 
situation. 
C. 
Materials Checked: 
1. 
Cal. Jur. (Accountants) 
2. 
West's Cal. Digest (Accountants) 


FEDERAL TAX CASES 
A. 
Goal: 
To find cases where a person, other than the 
individual signing the form, participated in 
its preparation and was held liable. 
B. 
Result: 
There are cases, but liability is imposed under 
26 USC Section 7206, which prohibits aiding, 
assisting, procuring, counseling or advising in 
the preparation of false returns. 
c. 
Materials Checked: 
USCA 


CALIFORNIA TAX CASES 
A. 
Goal: 
B. 
Resul t: 
To find cases where a filer's name is on an IRS 
filing, etc., but the actual preparer was held 
liable. 
No cases. 


SECURITIES CASES 
A. 
Goal: 
B. 
Result: 
To find cases where individuals, other than 
those with a specific legal duty, were held 
lliable for violating a statute. 
Sections 11 and 12(2} of the Securities Act. 
1. 
These provisions impose liability on 
certain persons (Section 11: 
The issuer; its 
directors or partners; underwriters and 
accountants who are named as having prepared or 
certified the registration statement) (Section 
12(2): 
The seller of a security). 
2. 
There are a number of cases which hold 
persons other than those who are listed in 
Sections 11 and 12, liable as aiders and 
abettors. 
This approach is based on the broad 
remedial nature of the 1933 Act, the perceived 
need for a liberal construction of the Act, and 
the idea that participants who substantially 
assist wrongdoing should not escape liability. 
(See In re Caesars Palace Securities Litigation 
(9173) 360 F. SUppa 366 (S.D.N.Y.». 
3. 
Unfortunately, California and the U.s. 
Supreme Court recently took a restrictive 
approach to these statutes and said that only 
persons named in a statute can be held liable. 
a. 
McFarland v. Memorex (1980) 493 
F. SUppa 631 (N.D. California). 
The court 
rejected a broad reading of Section 11. 
1) 
The court called the In re 
Caesars Palace approach a "minority 
position" and relied, instead, on In 
re Equity Funding, 416 F. SUppa 16-1--
(C.D. Cal. 1976) and In re Gap 
Stores, 457 F. SUppa 1135, 1143 (N.D. 
Cal. 1978) for a restrictive 
approach. 
b. 
Admiralty Fund v. Jones 677 F. 2d 
1289 (9th Cir. 1982). 
The court stated 
that it felt that the use of the a 
and 
abettor theory to hold 
rsons, other than 
those who are lis 
the statute, 
liable was doubtful in light of recent 
Supreme Court cases that prescribe a 
str 
t stat to 
construct 
oa 
t 
j 


expansion with tort and criminal 
theories. 
(Citing Admiralty Fund v. Hugh 
Johnson, 677 F.2d at 1311, N.12 (9th Cir. 
1982) and oting In re Gap Stores and In re 
Equuity Funding.) 
c. 
U.S. Supreme Court cases: 
1) 
Herman and MacLean v. 
Huddleston (1983) 
U.S. 
The 
Court strictly construed Section 11. 
2) 
Ernst & Ernst v. Hochfelder 
(1976) 425 u.s. 185, Touche Ross & 
Co. v. Redington (1979) 442 u.s. 
560: 
The Court required a strict 
statutory construction of the 
security acts to determine liability. 
Section lOb and Rule 10b-5 Cases. 
1. 
Section lOb and regulation 10b-5 makes 
it unlawful for "any person, directly or 
indirectly, by the use of any means or 
instrumentality of interstate commerce or of 
the mails, or of any facility of any national 
securities exchange: 
(a) 
To effect a short sale ••. 
(b) 
To use or employ ••• any 
manipulative or deceptive device •.• 
a. 
Secondary liability (aiding and 
abetting) has been imposed on persons who 
have not themselves engaged in the 
prohibited conduct, but who knew or should 
have known of the wrongdoing and rendered 
substantial assistance to the primary 
wrongdoer. 
b. 
The court, in Admiralty Fund v. 
Hugh Johnson Co., 67 F.2d 1301 (9th Cir. 
1982) refused to decide whether the use of 
and aiding and abetting theory was 
allowable. 
It cited recent u.S. Supreme 
Court cases as indicating that the use of 
this theory was doubtful. 
It also ci 
an art le: 
Secondary Liability Under 
Section lOeb) of the Securities Act of 
1934, Vol. 69 CLR 80, for its assertion 
that 
.S. 
erne Co 
t i 
taki 
tr 
t 
to 
i ie 


C. 
Materials Che 
ed: 
laws and that secondary liability should 
be abolished. 
c. 
The most recent u.s. Supreme 
Court case addressing this issue is Herman 
& MacLean v. Huddleston (1983) 
u.S. 
____ , 
it reserves the question of the 
propriety of allowing the use of an aiding 
and abetting theory for liability under 
lOb and lOb-So 
It recognizes the fact 
that many courts have allowed it. 
d. 
Until the u.S. Supreme Court 
addresses the question of secondary 
liability in lOb cases, California courts 
will probably continue to take a 
restrictive view and not impose liability. 
e. 
If the Supreme Court ultimately 
speaks in favor of secondary liability and 
makes the lower court cases useful: 
1) 
The lower courts have imposed 
liability based on the "broad policy 
objectives of the securities acts." 
(See Admiralty Fund v. Hugh Johnson & 
Co. (1982) 677 F.2d 1301, fn. 12.) 
2) 
The requirements for finding 
aiding and abetting liability are: 
a) 
a violation of lOb by the 
primary wrongdoer, 
b) 
knowledge of the 
violation by the aider and 
abettor, 
c) 
and substantial 
assistance by the aider. 
3) 
Normally an aider does not 
need to be under any type of legal 
duty. 
However, if liability is based 
upon inaction, the aider must have a 
duty of disclosure. 
1. 
California Corporations Co 
2. 
USCA. 
3. 
ts 
i 
nia Di 
st 
uritie 
ulat 
n. 
4 
. Jur. (Securit es Re 
ation) • 


FEDERAL CAMPAIGN CASES 
A. 
Goal: 
B. 
Result: 
Federal law imposes duties on treasurers 
similar to those imposed under our law and I 
was looking for cases addressing non-treasurer 
liability. 
No cases that I found. 
However, this might be 
worth contacting the FEC to discuss. 
C. 
Materials Checked: 
1. 
USCA. 
2. 
Campaign Practices Reports, Reference File 
and Current Reports.

sections.question_synthetic: The document presents a legal research memorandum investigating liability of persons acting as treasurers or filers without formal legal duties or responsibilities, examining case law across multiple domains including attorney conduct, accounting, federal and California tax law, securities law, and federal campaign finance.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal research memorandum, not a formal advice letter with traditional Q/A structure. No incoming question or FPPC conclusion sections present. Document contains research findings organized by legal area with goals, results, and materials checked for each category. Some text appears damaged or degraded in scanning (partial words visible). Not a direct response to an external inquiry.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The document presents a legal research memorandum investigating liability of persons acting as treasurers or filers without formal legal duties or responsibilities, examining case law across multiple domains including attorney conduct, accounting, federal and California tax law, securities law, and federal campaign finance.

embedding.summary: This is an internal FPPC memorandum from Janis McLean dated April 8, 1983, documenting a legal research project on liability of individuals acting as treasurers without formal duties. The research covers multiple areas of law (attorneys, accountants, federal/California tax, securities, and campaign finance) to determine when non-designated persons can be held liable for filing or preparation work.
